content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


July 3, 2001


Richard L. Poland
Law Offices of Richard L. Poland
333 W. Broadway, Suite 200
Long Beach, CA 90802

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-123

Dear Mr. Poland:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This advice is based upon the facts as provided in your request letter.² Please note that this letter should not be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).)

QUESTION

With respect to an ad in the Yellow Pages for your private law practice that also contains the words "VOTE" and "Office of the Long Beach City Prosecutor," an office for which you are a candidate, should the payment for this ad be made from your campaign bank account or from your private account for your law firm?

CONCLUSION

Because payment for the ad constitutes a campaign expenditure under the Act, it must be paid for from your campaign bank account.


_________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
² This advice is applicable only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and that all of the material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; § 83114.)

File No. A-01-123
Page No. 2

FACTS

You have provided a copy of your business advertisement that is running on the
cover of the Pacific Bell Smart Yellow Pages for the "Greater Long Beach & Lakewood
Area." This ad is ostensibly for your private law practice and is denominated as
"RICHARD L. POLAND ATTORNEY at LAW," but it also contains in large print, the
words "VOTE" and "Office of the Long Beach City Prosecutor." The word "VOTE"
appears as an acronym for several nonpolitical legal services provided by your private
law firm. The words describing these nonpolitical services are attached to the word,
"VOTE," which provides the first letter for each advertised service provided by your
firm. For example, the letter "V" in "VOTE" starts the phrase "VEHICULAR ACCIDENTS,"
and so forth. The letters in the word "VOTE" appear in such a manner that the acronym
is readily apparent. The words "Office of the Long Beach City Prosecutor" are also
readily apparent in the ad.

You are a candidate for the Office of Long Beach City Prosecutor for the election
in April of 2002. Your intent in running the above described advertisement was to
advertise your private law practice to potential clients, as well as to influence voters to
vote for you in the April 2002 election.

ANALYSIS

Under regulation 18225(a)(1), the definition of "expenditure" covers any payment
made for "the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters
for or against the nomination or election of a candidate." As your ad is aimed at swaying
voters to support your candidacy, it constitutes an expenditure.

Your payment for the ad would also be an expenditure, as a payment by a
candidate under Regulation 18225(a)(2)(A). A payment by a candidate is not an
"expenditure," where "it is clear from surrounding circumstances that the payment was
made for personal purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy...." (Ibid., emphasis
added.) In this case it cannot be said that the ad is unrelated to your candidacy. In fact,
there is no question that the payment for the ad is related to your candidacy, and again,
it constitutes an expenditure.³

Because the payment for the ad is a campaign expenditure, it must be made from
your campaign bank account under § 85201(e). To the extent that personal or business
funds would be used to pay for the ad, they would have to be deposited in the campaign
account prior to making the expenditure under § 85201(d). (See also Regulation
18215(b)(2).)

³ With the conclusion of the election, the payment for the ad will no longer be related to your
candidacy. (Rosenthal Advice Letter, No. I-93-167.)

File No. A-01-123
Page No. 3


If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By William L. Williams, Jr.,
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
